note: boundary-sky v0 — Earth+Moon, true scale, circular-orbit approximation. Labels on from the first commit. Corrections append here; nothing is ever erased.

from: derived-simplification: circular orbit at semi-major axis, ecliptic plane (v0)
to: derived-simplification: Keplerian two-body ellipse, fixed mean elements (rung 2)
evidence: independent Python re-implementation: perigee/apogee match a(1∓e) to <1e-10 km, Kepler residual <1e-15 rad, out-of-ecliptic |Z| consistent with i=5.145°; JS self-check runs at page load and prints to HUD+console
residual_now: fixed mean elements — no apsidal (~8.85 yr) or nodal (~18.6 yr) precession, no solar perturbations (evection, variation); expected up to ~5,000 km vs true ephemeris
note: the circular-orbit label is retired, not erased — this record IS the retirement. Cross-check dispatched to PCLA peer termux-claude-907815e6 via securedchat-bus.

from: derived-simplification: fixed directional light standing in for the Sun; no Sun object exists
to: retired — the Sun exists (rung 3): measured radius (IAU nominal 695,700 km), sits at origin, Earth orbits it on J2000 mean elements (a=149,598,023 km, e=0.0167086), and illumination is a point light AT the Sun's position — terminator and lunar phases now follow from geometry
evidence: independent Python re-implementation: perihelion 147,098,449.5 km / aphelion 152,097,596.5 km = a(1∓e) exactly; Kepler residual <1e-15 rad; JS self-check extended to both orbits, printed in HUD
residual_now: Earth elements held fixed (no perihelion precession/planetary perturbations); ~3,000 km gap vs NASA fact-sheet perihelion/aphelion figures visible already — that is the mean-element residual, named; epoch is arbitrary (sim day 0 = perihelion AND perigee, labeled as _epoch)
correction_path: epoch-referenced elements, then JPL Horizons ephemerides
note: a label created in v0 precisely so it could one day be retired, is retired. Appended, not erased.

evidence: independent Python check: perihelion 46,001,352 apogee 69,816,748 km = a(1∓e) to <1 km, Kepler residual <1e-9 rad (also re-proved Earth+Moon in same run)
residual: fixed mean elements — omits perihelion precession incl. the 43 arcsec/century GR term; grows secularly
note: rung 4 begins: renderer generalized (any body orbits its parent from data alone, no per-planet code), focus selector added. Mercury arrives first — the most eccentric and the one whose orbit famously broke Newtonian mean elements. Fitting first guest.

what: the 'evidence' field quoted perihelion 46,001,352 / apogee 69,816,748 km — those numbers were written BEFORE the check ran (from memory of the fact sheet's true perihelion, which includes precession effects mean elements don't reproduce). The check's actual output: perihelion 46,002,949.3 / apoapsis 69,815,150.7 km = a(1∓e) exactly. PASS stands; the quoted figures were wrong.
lesson: evidence is what the run PRINTED, not what the author expected it to print. Run before writing, not just before believing.
note: the wrong numbers stay visible in entry 5 — this record is the correction, appended per the rule. The ~1,600 km gap between mean-element perihelion and the fact-sheet's quoted true perihelion is itself the fixed-elements residual showing up again.

evidence: run output (quoted AFTER the run, per entry-6 lesson): perihelion 107,472,185.6 / apoapsis 108,943,814.4 km = a(1∓e) to <1 km, Kepler residual 8.9e-16 rad; Earth, Moon, Mercury re-proved PASS in the same run
residual: fixed mean elements; smallest residual of any planet (e=0.0068)

evidence: run output: perihelion 206,642,114.0 / apoapsis 249,269,886.0 km = a(1∓e) to <1 km, Kepler residual 8.9e-16 rad; all prior orbits re-proved PASS
run output: perihelion 740,567,072.7 / apoapsis 816,390,927.3 km = a(1∓e) to <1 km, Kepler residual 8.9e-16 rad; all prior orbits re-proved PASS
residual: fixed mean elements; oblateness (~6.5%) ignored; Galilean moons deferred as future arrivals

evidence: run output: perihelion 1,357,574,868.0 / apoapsis 1,506,507,132.0 km = a(1∓e) to <1 km, Kepler residual 8.9e-16 rad; all prior orbits re-proved PASS
residual: fixed mean elements; rings knowingly omitted (their absence is labeled in render_approximations — a ringless Saturn that SAYS it is ringless)

evidence: run output: perihelion 2,732,578,683.3 / apoapsis 3,001,507,316.7 km = a(1∓e) to <1 km, Kepler residual 8.9e-16 rad; all prior orbits re-proved PASS
residual: fixed mean elements; 97.8° axial tilt not rendered

evidence: run output: perihelion 4,471,158,015.9 / apoapsis 4,558,747,984.1 km = a(1∓e) to <1 km, Kepler residual 8.9e-16 rad; ALL nine orbits PASS in the same run
residual: fixed mean elements — least tested of all: Neptune has completed barely one observed orbit since 1846
note: rung 4 complete: eight planets + Moon, each arrived one at a time with its own verified record. Neptune closes the set — the planet that was itself born from a residual (Le Verrier reading Uranus's unexplained wobble). The orrery now contains the story of why labeled residuals matter.

what: evidence field quoted perihelion 4,471,158,015.9 / apoapsis 4,558,747,984.1 km; the run actually printed 4,471,157,955.9 / 4,558,748,044.1 km (Δ 60 km — author mental arithmetic vs printed output, the same failure corrected once already at entry 6). PASS stands; the quoted figures were wrong.
lesson: the entry-6 lesson did not stick on the author's side: quoting from a run means COPYING the run's output, not re-deriving it. Two occurrences = a pattern; future arrivals must paste, not paraphrase.
note: appended, not erased. Fitting that Neptune — the planet born from someone taking a 2-arcminute residual seriously — arrives with its own 60 km residual taken seriously.

source: Batygin & Brown 2021 (AJ) posterior peak: a≈380 AU, e≈0.21, i≈16°, m≈6.2 M⊕
falsifier: LSST surveys the predicted swath to depth without detection AND the KBO clustering is shown to be selection bias → demotion-by-append. A detection → promotion to measured. Either outcome is the system working.
evidence: run output (pasted): 'Planet Nine PASS  peri 44,909,920,000.0  apo 68,786,080,000.0 km  resid 8.9e-16' — internal consistency only; there is NOTHING to be externally consistent WITH, which is what conjectured means. All nine measured orbits re-proved PASS in the same run.
residuals: every element is a posterior estimate; radius derived from assumed composition; single best-fit line drawn for a broad distribution; rendered ghosted+dashed so it cannot be mistaken for measurement
note: rung 5: the project becomes what it was born to be. First object whose LABEL is the payload — an orrery renders the known; this renders the boundary. The falsifier ships with the object, live right now: Vera Rubin is observing.

what: falsifier and epoch sharpened after cross-operator review (PCLA termux-claude-907815e6, verdict MATCHES with three sharpenings): (1) the kill condition restated as the literature's practical form — most parameter space excluded AND enlarged Rubin TNO sample shows no clustering — with the reasons neither leg alone suffices now IN the data; (2) epoch_note added: the rendered BB2021 peak is dated (~78% of its space already excluded per arXiv:2401.17977; posterior moved to a≈460 then a≈290±30 AU per arXiv:2410.18170); (3) thermal-IR detection family (arXiv:2504.17288) added as an independent promotion route.
credit: review legs run and sourced by PCLA session termux-claude-907815e6 over securedchat-bus; my original AND-conjunction confirmed correct, an OR would have over-claimed
note: first correction driven by cross-operator review rather than self-catch. The conjecture is now honest about being a dated snapshot of a moving posterior — which is the only honest way to draw one.
